source_files: Elicit - How Common Medications Alter the Human Gut Microbiome - Report.pdf

findings.title: PPIs reduce alpha and beta diversity
findings.description: Across multiple human studies and meta-analyses, PPI exposure is repeatedly associated with altered gut community structure (beta diversity) and, in several datasets, lower alpha diversity (Shannon diversity p=0.01, species richness p=0.02). Short-term interventional studies sometimes report no within-individual alpha-diversity change.
cautions: Increased CDI (Clostridium difficile infection) risk
Reduced colonization resistance
Enrichment of taxa associated with enteric infections

findings.title: PPIs alter metabolic functions linked to pathogenic overgrowth
findings.description: PPI-associated functional shifts include increases in functional genes related to metabolic pathways and altered predicted metabolic genes/pathways. Functional evidence supports ecological changes predisposing to CDI and other enteric infections, with specific CDI-associated taxa (increased Enterococcaceae and Streptococcaceae, decreased Clostridiales) shifting during PPI use.
metabolic_changes: Bile acids often unchanged in some mechanistic trials
cautions: Ecological shifts predispose to CDI
Reduced colonization resistance to pathogens
Risk of enteric bacterial overgrowth

findings.title: Metformin produces minimal alpha diversity changes but detectable taxonomic shifts
findings.description: In randomized placebo-controlled contexts, metformin did not produce large shifts in overall alpha diversity or gross community separation, suggesting that metformin's microbiome effects may be more detectable at the level of specific taxa and functions. Overall microbiota differences were more dependent on inter-individual variation than intervention.
cautions: GI side effects linked to E. coli enrichment
Increased gas metabolism and fermentation-related genes
Higher E. coli abundance correlates with higher incidence of side effects

findings.title: Metformin enhances SCFA-related metabolism while producing gas-metabolism enrichment
findings.description: Multiple human studies report that metformin is associated with shifts in microbial functional potential and measured metabolites consistent with enhanced SCFA-related metabolism in some settings, alongside functional changes that may relate to gastrointestinal side effects. Functional shifts include enrichment of virulence factors and gas metabolism genes, largely attributable to increased Escherichia abundance.

metabolic_changes.context: production potential enhanced; measured increases at 4 months in men
production potential enhanced; measured increases at 4 months in men
fecal lactate increased with trend toward increased succinate
fecal bile acids did not show substantial changes; plasma bile acids increased
bsh genes increased after metformin exposure

findings.clinical_evidence: Fecal transfer from metformin-treated donors to germ-free mice improved glucose tolerance in recipients, supporting microbiota-mediated glucose regulation
cautions: GI side effects arise from microbial fermentation and gas production
E. coli enrichment linked to virulence factors and gas-metabolism genes
Increased E. coli abundance correlates with higher side effect incidence

findings.title: SSRIs show consistent beta-diversity differences but heterogeneous alpha-diversity effects
findings.description: Across human studies and reviews, antidepressant and SSRI exposure is most consistently associated with differences in beta diversity (community structure), while alpha diversity effects are mixed and sometimes absent depending on study design, population, and duration. Pooled longitudinal data showed significant differences in beta diversity but not alpha diversity after antidepressant treatment.

key_taxa.context: consistent depletion in antidepressant-treated MDD patients
consistent depletion in antidepressant-treated MDD patients

cautions: Responder vs non-responder microbiome signatures exist
Heterogeneous taxonomic changes depending on drug type
Treatment response not universally predictable from baseline microbiota

findings.title: SSRI treatment response linked to microbiome-derived biomarkers and metabolic pathways
findings.description: Functional results in SSRI/antidepressant studies frequently rely on predicted functional inference and multi-omics profiling, with studies reporting large-scale pathway shifts after treatment and differential metabolic pathway enrichment linked to clinical response. Baseline increased alpha diversity and higher baseline abundance of Roseburia and Faecalibacterium are associated with antidepressant response.

metabolic_changes.context: gut microbiota-derived tryptophan metabolite in escitalopram study

predictive_performance.description: Random forest ROC analysis on microbiota/metabolomics achieved high AUC values; baseline genes achieved AUC of 0.71 for remission prediction
predictive_performance.assessment: Remitters had higher baseline microbial richness and more complex community structure

cautions: Responder vs non-responder microbiome signatures present
Multiple microbiome pathways altered; response heterogeneous
Predictive models require baseline characterization

findings.title: Statins show modest diversity effects with enterotype-dependent and bile acid-linked outcomes
findings.description: Human studies suggest statins have small or inconsistent associations with alpha diversity and modest but statistically detectable associations with beta diversity, with evidence that statin effects interact with baseline microbiome states (e.g., enterotypes). Statin-associated taxonomic signals are described in terms of microbiome configurations linked to SCFA-producing commensals and dysbiosis-related enterotypes rather than a single universal signature.

key_taxa.context: SCFA-producer; increased in some response groups
potentially pathogenic; modulated downward in ACS
linked to bile acid axis dysregulation

findings.enterotype_effects: Dysbiosis-related Bac.2 enterotype characterized by decreased alpha diversity and depletion of SCFA-producing commensals; statin effects vary by baseline enterotype
cautions: Glucose intolerance risk via microbiome-mediated mechanism
Enterotype-dependent response variability
Bile acid axis involvement in hyperglycemic effects
Heterogeneous lipid-lowering response depending on microbiome state

findings.title: Statins modulate bile acids and choline/betaine-TMA pathway, affecting metabolic outcomes
findings.description: Statins show comparatively strong links to microbiome-associated metabolic axes, particularly bile acids and microbial functions related to the choline/betaine-TMA pathway, which may help explain heterogeneity in metabolic outcomes and adverse effects. Statin-induced hyperglycemic effects are proposed to involve a Clostridium-bile acid axis.

metabolic_changes.context: both serum and fecal bile acids affected
rosuvastatin trial; functions related to cellular transport and metabolism reduced
despite betaine increases in rosuvastatin trial

findings.clinical_mechanism: Atorvastatin-induced hyperglycemia attributed to Clostridium-bile acid axis dysregulation; UDCA proposed as adjuvant strategy
cautions: Glucose intolerance risk via bile acid-mediated mechanism
Enterotype-dependent response variability
Type 2 diabetes risk associated with specific taxa (e.g., Ruminococcus torques, Blautia obeum)
Lipid-lowering response heterogeneity based on microbiome features

findings.title: Corticosteroid effects on microbiome are route- and context-dependent
findings.description: Human evidence suggests corticosteroid effects on microbiome diversity are context- and route-dependent. Intramuscular dosing produces minimal microbiota changes, while oral or high-dose regimens show detectable compositional and functional shifts. Minimal changes observed in 7-day trials with intramuscular treatment; significant shifts reported in pediatric IBD and Graves' ophthalmopathy patients receiving high-dose glucocorticoids.

key_taxa.context: oral glucocorticoid treatment; minimal with IM
oral glucocorticoid treatment; minimal with IM

findings.route_effects: Intramuscular treatment produces minimal microbiota changes; oral treatment produces detectable shifts in composition and function
cautions: Route-dependent effects (oral vs IM); IM shows minimal effects
High-dose glucocorticoids show stronger microbiota effects
Context-dependent (e.g., IBD patients, Graves' ophthalmopathy)
Reduced SCFA-producing capacity in some patient populations

findings.title: Corticosteroids reduce SCFA-producing capacity and alter metabolic pathways
findings.description: Several corticosteroid studies implicate changes in microbial metabolic pathways and reduced SCFA-related functional capacity, with some cohorts reporting altered gut metabolites. High-dose glucocorticoids decrease SCFA-producing ability and increase 5-hydroxytryptamine (serotonin). Integrated multi-omics work describes the osrABC reductive steroid hormone pathway, and experimental evidence in gnotobiotic mice shows colonization can decrease prednisolone bioavailability.

metabolic_changes.context: selection for steroid-processing bacterial capacity; 9.6% vs 2.4% osrA homolog prevalence

findings.drug_bioavailability_effect: Colonization with steroid-reducing bacteria can decrease prednisolone bioavailability (demonstrated in gnotobiotic mice)
cautions: SCFA-producing capacity reduced, especially with high-dose exposure
Route-dependent effects; oral more pronounced than IM
Altered metabolic pathways may relate to known side effects (hypertension, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis)
Microbiome may modulate corticosteroid bioavailability

findings.title: Oral contraceptives frequently show minimal or null effects on diversity
findings.description: Across human studies, oral contraceptive (OC) use is frequently reported to have minimal or null effects on overall gut microbiome diversity and broad community composition, though some studies find cycle- or context-dependent richness differences. Longitudinal studies show no changes in diversity or composition over time.

key_taxa.context: in some OC user cohorts; null in others
in some OC user cohorts; null in others
in some OC user cohorts; null in others
lifetime OC use linked to decreased Clostridia ASVs
16 of 25 Firmicutes ASVs belonged to Clostridia; decreased with lifetime OC duration

findings.cycle_effects: Some studies report cycle-dependent effects on species richness (day 21) but not composition
cautions: Minimal or null effects in most studies
Context-dependent effects when present
Limited direct metabolite evidence
Heterogeneous results across cohorts

findings.title: Oral contraceptives show inferred functional enrichments but lack direct metabolite assessment
findings.description: OC studies rarely quantify fecal SCFAs, bile acids, or comprehensive metabolomic endpoints, so functional claims often rest on inferred pathways or physiological proxies such as breath gas production. Initiation of OC use associated with enrichments in microbial functions including amino-acid biosynthesis and peptidoglycan biosynthesis. Clinical implications are largely indirect with discussion of symptom relevance via gas production rather than direct infection or efficacy outcomes.

inferred_functions.association: correlated with OC use and menstrual cycle
inferred_functions.limitation: physiological proxy rather than direct SCFA quantification

clinical_markers.finding: Higher lactulose-to-rhamnose ratio in OC users vs other groups
Permeability not impacted by pill cycle in one study

cautions: Minimal or null effects in most studies
Limited direct functional metabolite endpoints
Inferred functions lack direct validation
No established microbiome-mediated clinical endpoints (infection, treatment efficacy)

findings.title: Polypharmacy accumulates medication signals with additive dysbiotic effects
findings.description: Medication signals accumulate under polypharmacy, where additive drug effects are associated with altered community structure, enrichment of upper gastrointestinal/oral taxa, depletion of SCFA producers, and reduced SCFA metabolism. Polypharmacy-exposed individuals show increased Streptococcus and Lactobacillus species with depletion of the Shannon index and SCFA-producing capacity.

key_effects.consequence: reduced short-chain fatty acid production potential

key_effects.context: significant correlation with non-antibiotic drugs under polypharmacy

key_effects.consequence: harbor more abundant upper-GI species and pathobionts

findings.mechanistic_summary: SCFA depletion can increase gut permeability and heighten CDI risk; elevated gut pH under PPI use facilitates overgrowth of taxa like Streptococcus and Rothia and pathogenic bacteria like C. difficile
cautions: Additive effects of multiple medications
Reduced SCFA production and increased permeability
Increased antimicrobial resistance potential
Enhanced colonization by pathobionts

summary.overview: This report synthesizes evidence from large human cohorts and targeted interventional studies on medication-microbiome interactions across six major drug classes: PPIs, metformin, SSRIs/antidepressants, statins, corticosteroids, and oral contraceptives.
key_patterns: Strong and consistent compositional signatures for PPIs with oral-taxa enrichment and SCFA-producer depletion
Metformin produces prominent Escherichia/Shigella shifts linked to glycemic benefit but GI side effects
SSRIs show heterogeneous taxonomy but consistent beta-diversity differences with treatment-response biomarker potential
Statins demonstrate modest diversity effects but strong metabolic axis links (bile acids, choline-TMA) to glucose intolerance
Corticosteroids show route-dependent effects (oral vs IM) with SCFA-capacity reduction
Oral contraceptives frequently show minimal effects on diversity in most studies
Polypharmacy shows additive dysbiotic effects with reduced SCFA metabolism and increased pathobiont colonization
summary.clinical_actionability: PPIs and metformin have the most direct clinical implications (CDI risk and GI side effects respectively); antidepressants and statins offer microbiome-based biomarker potential; corticosteroids show mechanistic links to side effects; oral contraceptives show minimal microbiome relevance in most settings
